Data Structures · Linked Lists · Quiz 19

Delete a node without passing trail.

A Brilliant-style mini lesson built from the material in ds-lecture7.pdf. Work through the idea, animate the pointer updates, and then write the final function yourself.

Start lesson Open PDF
Lesson progress 0 / 4 complete
Quiz snapshot
Referenced slides
  • Slide 10: original delete(first, trail, x)
  • Slide 11: Quiz 19 prompt

The lesson below paraphrases and visualizes the same pointer rules from the PDF, then asks you to reconstruct the missing predecessor search.

Source material

Reference panel from the PDF

Slide 10: original delete logic

void delete(listPointer *first, listPointer trail, listPointer x)
{
    if (trail)
        trail->link = x->link;
    else
        *first = (*first)->link;
    free(x);
}

This version works because the caller already knows the node right before x. Quiz 19 removes that shortcut.

Slide 11: the actual quiz

“Modify delete() so that it takes only two arguments, first and x without trail.”

That means the function itself must find the predecessor unless x is already the first node.

Step 1

Warm-up: what changed?

The old function could delete x in constant time after the caller passed trail. Without trail, what extra work must the function do?

Step 2

Pointer simulator

Pick a target node and watch how the function behaves. The simulation follows the exact rule implied by the PDF: special-case the head, otherwise search for the predecessor and bypass the target. This version also shows how the list sits in memory as separate blocks, each with a data field and a link field that stores the address of the next node.

Linked-list nodes do not need to be stored in adjacent memory. The order comes from pointers, not physical position. So first stores the address of the first block, x stores the address of the node to delete, and trail is the temporary pointer we move until trail->link == x.

Memory blocks

Detached / Freed Target Block

Pointer meaning

  • first stores the address of the head node.
  • x stores the address of the node we want to delete.
  • trail chases the list to find the predecessor of x.
  • link inside each block stores the address of the next node, or NULL.
Choose a target node, then run the simulation.
Observation: when x is not the head, the function must search for the node right before it.

Step 3

Build the algorithm

Put the strategy in order. Click the steps in the correct sequence.

Your sequence

    Step 4

    Write the function

    Use the PDF logic and your simulator results to write a valid version of delete(listPointer *first, listPointer x).

    What the checker looks for

    • Handle the head case separately.
    • Search for the predecessor of x.
    • Reconnect the list around x.
    • Release the removed node with free(x).

    Reference solution

    void delete(listPointer *first, listPointer x) {
    listPointer trail;

    if (!first || !*first || !x) return;

    if (*first == x) {
        *first = (*first)->link;
        free(x);
        return;
    }

    for (trail = *first; trail && trail->link != x; trail = trail->link)
        ;

    if (trail) {
        trail->link = x->link;
        free(x);
    }
}
